title: Comprehensive Guide Classification R INFS3822
body:
INFS3822 Comprehensive Guide in R including all steps required to build ML Classification Models

This guide summarises all the steps you learned during weeks 3 and 4 including data loading, preprocessing (data cleaning and transformation including the conversion of categorical variables into dummy variables), feature selection (using Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap), splitting data into training and testing datasets, model training and testing to assess the performance of the models. This guide including the steps requires for building ML models for classification problems using logistic regression, decision tree and neural network in R. First, upload the file data_online_retail_customers.csv into this notebook on Google Colab. Once the dataset is uploaded, you can conduct the following steps.

1 Loading Libraries The following libraries are the ones used during weeks 3 and 4 that cover the required libraries for logistic regression, decision tree, and neural networks. # install all R packages needed install . packages ( " gridExtra " ) install . packages ( " dplyr " ) install . packages ( " corrplot " ) install . packages ( " rpart . plot " ) install . packages ( " keras " ) install . packages ( " DALEX " )

library ( ggplot2 ) library ( dplyr ) library ( lubridate ) library ( gridExtra ) library ( corrplot ) library ( rpart ) library ( rpart . plot ) library ( keras ) library ( DALEX )

2 Loading the Data from a CSV File # Load the data data <- read . csv ( " data _ online _ retail _ customers . csv " )

3 Data Cleaning # Check for missing values missing _ values <- is . na ( data ) print ( sum ( missing _ values ) ) [1] 0

# Remove rows with missing values , if any data <- na . omit ( data )

4 Data Transformation # Check the data type of all variables summary ( data )

Customer_ID Age Gender Annual_Income Min. : 1.0 Min. :18.00 Length:676 Min. : 20.01 1st Qu.:169.8 1st Qu.:31.00 Class :character 1st Qu.: 70.94 Median :338.5 Median :45.00 Mode :character Median :117.86 Mean :338.5 Mean :44.21 Mean :114.81 3rd Qu.:507.2 3rd Qu.:57.00 3rd Qu.:162.44 Max. :676.0 Max. :69.00 Max. :199.73 Total_Spend Years_as_Customer Num_of_Purchases Num_of_Returns Min. : 150 Min. : 1.000 Min. : 1.00 Min. :0.000 1st Qu.: 2819 1st Qu.: 5.000 1st Qu.:25.00 1st Qu.:2.000 Median : 4886 Median : 9.000 Median :49.00 Median :5.000 Mean : 5093 Mean : 9.722 Mean :50.17 Mean :4.657 3rd Qu.: 7609 3rd Qu.:14.000 3rd Qu.:76.00 3rd Qu.:7.000 Max. :10000 Max. :19.000 Max. :99.00 Max. :9.000 Num_of_Support_Contacts Satisfaction_Score Last_Purchase_Days_Ago Min. :0.000 Min. :1.000 Min. : 1.00 1st Qu.:1.000 1st Qu.:2.000 1st Qu.: 94.75 Median :2.000 Median :3.000 Median :176.00 Mean :1.945 Mean :3.013 Mean :183.31 3rd Qu.:3.000 3rd Qu.:4.000 3rd Qu.:273.00 Max. :4.000 Max. :5.000 Max. :364.00 Email_Opt_In Promotion_Response Churned Mode :logical Length:676 Mode :logical FALSE:326 Class :character FALSE:328 TRUE :350 Mode :character TRUE :348

# Convert categorical columns ( Class : character ) into ' factor ' type ( i . e . category ) data $ Gender = as . factor ( data $ Gender ) data $ Promotion _ Response = as . factor ( data $ Promotion _ Response )

Further data transformations Uncomment the lines you need by removing the # Modify the code to fit your needs # In this dataset , the target variable is Churned , and it is of type ' logical ' ( i . e . boolean ) . # If the target variable is binary ( e . g . Yes / No , Good / Bad , Success / Fail ) , make sure you convert it into boolean variable as follows : # data $ Churned = data $ Churned == 'Yes '

# Convert date columns into datetime based on your objectives . # For further details , refer to Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering # data $ vdate <- as . Date ( data $ vdate , format = "% m / % d / % Y ") # for dates in the format m / d / yyyy # data $ vdate <- as . Date ( data $ vdate , format = "% d / % m / % Y ") # for dates in the format d / m / yyyy

# If you have a variable representing a date , you can transform it into a categorical variable ( Think of what would be the categories that are relevant to your analysis objectives ) # For further details , refer to Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering # Create a categorical variable for seasons # get _ season <- function ( date ) { # month <- month ( date ) # if ( month % in % c (12 , 1 , 2) ) { # return ( ' Winter ') # } else if ( month % in % c (3 , 4 , 5) ) { # return ( ' Spring ') # } else if ( month % in % c (6 , 7 , 8) ) { # return ( ' Summer ') # } else { # return ( ' Autumn ') # } # } #
# data $ season <- sapply ( data $ vdate , get _ season ) # data $ season <- as . factor ( data $ season )

# You can transform some data values . For example , convert '5+ ' to numeric 5 in the ' rcount ' column ( Week4 _ Pre _ Seminar _ Using _ R _ for _ Clustering ) # data [ which ( data $ rcount == "5+") ,] $ rcount = "5" # data $ rcount <- as . integer ( data $ rcount )

# Scale the numeric input data # Identify numeric columns numeric _ columns <- sapply ( data , is . numeric )

# Scale numeric columns data [ , numeric _ columns ] <- scale ( data [ , numeric _ columns ])

5 Feature Selection # Split the data into inputs ( X as features ) and outputs ( y as target variable ) . # Initial feature selection ( i . e . selecting which variables should be included in X ) is required here as follows .

# You can exclude variables that represent unique identifiers because they don ' t have any impact on the target variable . # You can exclude variables that are text descriptions and not useful as categories . # You can exclude categorical variables that have too many possible values , as these would generate too many dummy variables . A model with too many variables cannot perform well and sometimes cause the machine to run out of memory . This would also help you focus on the most important variables . X <- data _ selected <- data [ , ! ( names ( data ) % in % c ( " Customer _ ID " , " Churned " ) ) ] y <- data _ selected <- data $ Churned

Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap # Determine the type of the target variable target _ is _ numeric <- is . numeric ( y ) target _ is _ categorical <- is . factor ( y ) target _ is _ logical <- is . logical ( y )

target <- " Churned "

# Plot each feature against the target variable using appropriate plots in a grid num _ features <- ncol ( X ) num _ cols <- 3 num _ rows <- ceiling ( num _ features / num _ cols ) # Ceiling division

# Create an empty list to store plots plots <- list ()

# Plot each feature against the target variable for ( i in seq _ len ( num _ features ) ) { feature <- colnames ( X ) [ i ] feature _ is _ numeric <- is . numeric ( X [[ feature ]]) feature _ is _ categorical <- is . factor ( X [[ feature ]])

# Select appropriate plot if (( target _ is _ categorical || target _ is _ logical ) & & feature _ is _ numeric ) { p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = " y " , y = feature ) ) + geom _ boxplot () + labs ( title = paste ( " Box Plot of " , feature , " by " , target ) , x = target , y = feature ) } else if ( target _ is _ numeric & & feature _ is _ numeric ) { p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , y = " y " ) ) + geom _ point () + labs ( title = paste ( " Scatter Plot of " , feature , " vs " , target ) , x = feature , y = target ) } else if (( target _ is _ categorical || target _ is _ logical ) & & feature _ is _ categorical ) { p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , fill = " y " ) ) + geom _ bar ( position = " dodge " ) + labs ( title = paste ( " Count Plot of " , feature , " by " , target ) , x = feature , fill = target ) } else if ( target _ is _ numeric & & feature _ is _ categorical ) { p <- ggplot ( data . frame (X , y ) , aes _ string ( x = feature , y = " y " ) ) + geom _ boxplot () + labs ( title = paste ( " Box Plot of " , target , " by " , feature ) , x = feature , y = target ) } else {

p <- NULL }

if ( ! is . null ( p ) ) { plots [[ i ]] <- p } }

# Arrange plots in a grid do . call ( grid . arrange , c ( plots , ncol = num _ cols ) )

# Convert categorical variables into dummy variables to include individual categories as variables . Please make sure that you do this before constructing the correlation heatmap . This would help you identify the most important features to be included in your models . It is also important to do this before training the models . X <- as . data . frame ( model . matrix ( ~ . , data = X ) ) [ , -1]

# Let ' s plot the correlation matrix heatmap to check which variables are important ( i . e . correlated with the target variable ) . # Combine X and y into one data frame data2 <- cbind (X , data . frame ( Churned = y ) )

# Convert non - numeric columns to numeric ( if applicable ) data2 <- data . frame ( lapply ( data2 , function ( x ) { if ( is . factor ( x ) ) as . numeric ( as . factor ( x ) ) else if ( is . character ( x ) ) as . numeric ( as . factor ( x ) ) else x }) )

# Compute the correlation matrix corr _ matrix <- cor ( data2 )

# Plot the heatmap options ( repr . plot . width = 15 , repr . plot . height = 15) # Set the plot size corrplot ( corr _ matrix , method = " color " , tl . col = " black " , tl . cex = 0.8 , number . cex = 0.7 , addCoef . col = " black " , col = colorRampPalette ( c ( " blue " , " white " , " red " ) ) (200) , title = " Correlation Matrix Heatmap " , mar = c (0 , 0 , 1 , 0) )

# Further feature selection based on the results of pair plots and / or correlation matrix heatmap . # You can exclude variables that are not correlated with the targe variable . # When 2 variables ( other than the target variable ) are highly correlated to each other , you can remove one of them to address multi - colinearity problem ( keep the one that is highly correlated with the target variable ) . X <- data _ selected <- X [ , ! ( names ( X ) % in % c ( " Age " , " Promotion _ Response _ Responded " ) )]

6 Split the data into training and testing sets # Split the data into training and testing sets set . seed (123) # For reproducibility n <- nrow ( X ) splitIndex <- sample (1: n , size = round (0.8 * n ) , replace = FALSE ) X _ train <- X [ splitIndex ,] y _ train <- y [ splitIndex ] X _ test <- X [ - splitIndex ,] y _ test <- y [ - splitIndex ]

7 Train and test a Logistic Regression Model # Train a logistic regression model on the training data . # If your dataset is highly imbalanced , the model might predict the majority class for all instances . # An imbalanced dataset for a binary target variable like " Churn " means that the number of cases of one class ( customers who churn ) is much smaller or much larger than the number of cases of the other class ( customers who don ' t churn ) . # You can enable weight adjustment to adjust the class weights in the logistic regression model to give more importance to the minority class .

# Calculate weights inversely proportional to class frequencies class _ counts <- table ( y _ train ) total _ count <- length ( y _ train ) weights <- ifelse ( y _ train == TRUE , total _ count / (2 * class _ counts [2]) , total _ count / (2 * class _ counts [1]) )

# Train the logistic regression model with weights model <- glm ( y _ train ~ . , data = X _ train , family = binomial () , weights = weights )

# Make predictions on the testing data y _ pred <- predict ( model , newdata = X _ test , type = " response " ) y _ pred <- ifelse ( y _ pred > 0.5 , 1 , 0)

# Calculate the accuracy of the model accuracy <- mean ( y _ pred == y _ test ) message ( " Accuracy : " , accuracy )

# Create a confusion matrix conf _ matrix <- table ( y _ test , y _ pred )

# Extract true positives , false positives , true negatives , and false negatives TP <- conf _ matrix [2 , 2] FP <- conf _ matrix [1 , 2] TN <- conf _ matrix [1 , 1] FN <- conf _ matrix [2 , 1]

# Calculate precision , recall , and F1 score precision <- TP / ( TP + FP ) recall <- TP / ( TP + FN ) f1 _ score <- 2 * ( precision * recall ) / ( precision + recall )

message ( " Precision : " , precision ) message ( " Recall : " , recall ) message ( " F1 Score : " , f1 _ score )

# Display feature coefficients summary ( model )

Warning message in eval(family$initialize): “non-integer #successes in a binomial glm!” Accuracy: 0.533333333333333

Precision: 0.507692307692308

Recall: 0.515625

F1 Score: 0.511627906976744

Call: glm(formula = y_train ~ ., family = binomial(), data = X_train, weights = weights)

Coefficients: Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|) (Intercept) 0.119782 0.193633 0.619 0.5362 GenderMale 0.017224 0.175559 0.098 0.9218 Annual_Income 0.101817 0.087532 1.163 0.2447 Total_Spend 0.093561 0.088016 1.063 0.2878 Years_as_Customer 0.124258 0.088950 1.397 0.1624 Num_of_Purchases -0.207281 0.087845 -2.360 0.0183 * Num_of_Returns 0.045715 0.087909 0.520 0.6031 Num_of_Support_Contacts -0.018501 0.087934 -0.210 0.8334 Satisfaction_Score 0.055442 0.087120 0.636 0.5245 Last_Purchase_Days_Ago 0.006319 0.088219 0.072 0.9429 Email_Opt_InTRUE -0.169943 0.174663 -0.973 0.3306 Promotion_ResponseResponded 0.024604 0.215738 0.114 0.9092 Promotion_ResponseUnsubscribed -0.139052 0.215409 -0.646 0.5186

Signif. codes: 0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

Null deviance: 749.99 on 540 degrees of freedom Residual deviance: 737.90 on 528 degrees of freedom AIC: 763.88

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Exercise 2 Logistic Regression - R” provided on Moodle.

8 Train a Decision Tree Model # Train a decision tree model on the training data # If your dataset is highly imbalanced , the model might predict the majority class for all instances . # An imbalanced dataset for a binary target variable like " Churn " means that the number of cases of one class ( customers who churn ) is much smaller or much larger than the number of cases of the other class ( customers who don ' t churn ) . # You can enable weight adjustment to adjust the class weights in the decision tree model to give more importance to the minority class . # Calculate weights inversely proportional to class frequencies class _ counts <- table ( y _ train ) total _ count <- sum ( class _ counts ) class _ weights <- total _ count / ( length ( class _ counts ) * class _ counts )

# Normalize the class weights class _ weights <- class _ weights / sum ( class _ weights )

# Train a decision tree model on the training data with weights model <- rpart ( y _ train ~ . , data = X _ train , method = " class " , parms = list ( prior = class _ weights ) )

# Make predictions on the testing data y _ pred <- predict ( model , newdata = X _ test , type = " class " )

# Calculate the accuracy of the model accuracy <- mean ( y _ pred == y _ test ) message ( " Accuracy : " , accuracy )

# Create a confusion matrix conf _ matrix <- table ( y _ test , y _ pred )

# Extract true positives , false positives , true negatives , and false negatives TP <- conf _ matrix [2 , 2] FP <- conf _ matrix [1 , 2] TN <- conf _ matrix [1 , 1] FN <- conf _ matrix [2 , 1]

# Calculate precision , recall , and F1 score

precision <- TP / ( TP + FP ) recall <- TP / ( TP + FN ) f1 _ score <- 2 * ( precision * recall ) / ( precision + recall )

message ( " Precision : " , precision ) message ( " Recall : " , recall ) message ( " F1 Score : " , f1 _ score )

# Plot the decision tree rpart . plot ( model )

# Display the decision tree strcutrue in text form ( easier to read ) message ( " Tree Structure : " ) print ( model )

# Display the Feature Importance Scores importance <- model $ variable . importance message ( " Feature Importance Scores : " ) print ( importance )

179) GenderMale< 0.5 25 8.8404670 TRUE (0.36494256 0.63505744) 358) Last_Purchase_Days_Ago>=1.302221 7 1.8098590 FALSE (0.75326218 0.24673782) * 359) Last_Purchase_Days_Ago< 1.302221 18 3.3151750 TRUE (0.19629059 0.80370941) * 45) Annual_Income< -0.355723 31 11.0505800 TRUE (0.36768966 0.63231034) * 23) Satisfaction_Score>=-0.3677829 224 104.9805000 TRUE (0.47349008 0.52650992) 46) Num_of_Purchases>=1.645043 7 0.9049296 FALSE (0.87990763 0.12009237) * 47) Num_of_Purchases< 1.645043 217 98.3501900 TRUE (0.45919158 0.54080842) 94) Num_of_Returns< -1.439783 17 5.4295770 FALSE (0.69124198 0.30875802) * 95) Num_of_Returns>=-1.439783 200 86.1945500 TRUE (0.43843501 0.56156499) 190) Total_Spend< -1.376374 21 7.2394370 FALSE (0.66492149 0.33507851) * 191) Total_Spend>=-1.376374 179 71.8287900 TRUE (0.41047191 0.58952809) 382) Annual_Income>=0.6205833 76 37.1021100 FALSE (0.51039158 0.48960842) 764) Total_Spend>=-0.4844703 60 25.3380300 FALSE (0.58256884 0.41743116) 1528) Years_as_Customer< 1.042354 46 15.3838000 FALSE (0.67565587 0.32434413) * 1529) Years_as_Customer>=1.042354 14 3.3151750 TRUE (0.24983609 0.75016391) * 765) Total_Spend< -0.4844703 16 3.3151750 TRUE (0.21984999 0.78015001) * 383) Annual_Income< 0.6205833 103 33.1517500 TRUE (0.33415193 0.66584807) 766) Annual_Income< -0.3658557 41 19.8910500 TRUE (0.48867040 0.51132960) 1532) Total_Spend< 1.009106 31 12.6690100 FALSE (0.59723378 0.40276622) 3064) Last_Purchase_Days_Ago>=-0.3862995 19 4.5246480 FALSE (0.77371630 0.22628370) * 3065) Last_Purchase_Days_Ago< -0.3862995 12 3.3151750 TRUE (0.28929387 0.71070613) * 1533) Total_Spend>=1.009106 10 1.1050580 TRUE (0.11947320

0.88052680) * 767) Annual_Income>=-0.3658557 62 13.2607000 TRUE (0.22665082 0.77334918) * 3) Num_of_Purchases< -0.9269722 118 46.4124500 TRUE (0.40293099 0.59706901) 6) Num_of_Returns< 0.9847911 95 43.0972800 TRUE (0.45958990 0.54041010) 12) Annual_Income< 0.2057985 54 23.5281700 FALSE (0.56805115 0.43194885) 24) Annual_Income>=-0.613263 22 5.4295770 FALSE (0.76506026 0.23493974) * 25) Annual_Income< -0.613263 32 13.2607000 TRUE (0.42286351 0.57713649) 50) Num_of_Support_Contacts>=-1.036267 23 10.8591500 FALSE (0.52816638 0.47183362) 100) Num_of_Purchases>=-1.465705 14 4.5246480 FALSE (0.68731211 0.31268789) * 101) Num_of_Purchases< -1.465705 9 2.2101170 TRUE (0.25865583 0.74134417) * 51) Num_of_Support_Contacts< -1.036267 9 1.1050580 TRUE (0.13242963 0.86757037) * 13) Annual_Income>=0.2057985 41 12.1556400 TRUE (0.30927609 0.69072391) *

To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 4 Further Hands-On Practice - Decision Tree in R” provided on Moodle.

9 Train a Neural Network Model # Convert boolean columns in X _ train to numeric (0 or 1) # Please ensure that all booleans are converted into numeric (0 or 1) , as TensorFlow and Keras neural networks expect numeric values as input . X _ train <- as . data . frame ( lapply ( X _ train , function ( x ) if ( is . logical ( x ) ) as . numeric ( x ) else x ) ) y _ train = if ( is . logical ( y _ train ) ) as . numeric ( y _ train ) else y _ train

X _ test <- as . data . frame ( lapply ( X _ test , function ( x ) if ( is . logical ( x ) ) as . numeric ( x ) else x ) ) y _ test = if ( is . logical ( y _ test ) ) as . numeric ( y _ test ) else y _ test

# If your dataset is highly imbalanced , the model might predict the majority class for all instances . # An imbalanced dataset for a binary target variable like " Churn " means that the number of cases of one class ( customers who churn ) is much smaller or much larger than the number of cases of the other class ( customers who don ' t churn ) . # You can enable Class Weight Adjustment ( class _ weight = class _ weights ) to adjust the class weights in the neural network model to give more importance to the minority class . # Calculate class weights class _ weights <- list ( " 0 " = sum ( y _ train == 1) / length ( y _ train ) , " 1 " = sum ( y _ train == 0) / length ( y _ train ) )

# Define the model model <- keras _ model _ sequential () % >% layer _ dense ( units = 12 , input _ shape = ncol ( X _ train ) , activation = ' relu ') % >% layer _ dense ( units = 6 , activation = ' relu ') % >% layer _ dense ( units = 1 , activation = ' sigmoid ')

# Compile the model model % >% compile ( loss = ' binary _ crossentropy ' , optimizer = ' adam ' , metrics = c ( ' accuracy ') )

# Train the model model % >% fit ( as . matrix ( X _ train ) , y _ train , epochs = 5 , batch _ size = 200 , class _ weight = class _ weights )

# Evaluate the model score <- model % >% evaluate ( as . matrix ( X _ test ) , y _ test , verbose = 0) score [ " accuracy " ]

# Make class predictions with the model predictions <- ifelse ( model % >% predict ( as . matrix ( X _ test ) ) > 0.5 , 1 , 0)

# Print confusion matrix conf _ matrix <- table ( Predicted = predictions , Actual = y _ test ) print ( conf _ matrix )

# Calculate the accuracy , precision , sensitivity , fscore and specificity of the predictions . tp <- conf _ matrix [2 , 2] tn <- conf _ matrix [1 , 1] fp <- conf _ matrix [2 , 1] fn <- conf _ matrix [1 , 2]

precision <- tp / ( tp + fp ) recall <- tp / ( tp + fn )

f1 <- 2 * precision * recall / ( precision + recall )

cat ( ' Precision : ' , precision , '\ n ') cat ( ' Recall : ' , recall , '\ n ') cat ( ' F1 Score : ' , f1 , '\ n ')

# Evaluate feature importance using Permutation Importance # Define a prediction function for DALEX predict _ function <- function ( model , newdata ) { as . numeric ( predict ( model , as . matrix ( newdata ) ) ) }

# Create an explainer object for DALEX explainer <- DALEX :: explain ( model , data = as . data . frame ( X _ test ) , y = as . numeric ( y _ test ) , predict _ function = predict _ function , label = " Neural Network " )

# Calculate feature importances importance <- DALEX :: model _ parts ( explainer )

# Print feature importances print ( importance )

accuracy: 0.496296286582947 Actual Predicted 0 1 0 33 30 1 38 34 Precision: 0.4722222 Recall: 0.53125 F1 Score: 0.5 Preparation of a new explainer is initiated -> model label : Neural Network -> data : 135 rows 12 cols -> target variable : 135 values -> predict function : predict_function -> predicted values : No value for predict function target column. To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Deep Dive - Deep Learning in R” provided on Moodle.
